columns.name: id

bus.description: Table containing business unit (BU) options. Every product at NVIDIA belongs a particular business unit, or BU

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the business unit, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the business unit entry was created, defaults to now()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the business unit entry, references users(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the business unit, must be unique

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the business unit

products.description: Table containing product options. A business unit is responsible for multiple products. For example, the 'GPU' business unit has 'Quadro' and 'Geforce', to name a few.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the product, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the product was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the product, references users(id)

columns.name: bu_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the business unit associated with the product, references bus(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the product, must be unique

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the product

pcodes.description: Table containing product code options. A product can have multiple product codes. For example, the 'Quadro' product has 'PG150' and 'PG400' to name a few.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the product code, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the product code was created, defaults to now()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the product code, references users(id)

columns.name: product_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the product associated with the product code, references products(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with name

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the product code. Part of a composite unique constraint with product_id

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the product code

boms.description: Table containing Bill of Materials (BOM) information. A pcode can have multiple BOMs. BOMs look something like '699-5G147-0520-RC2' or 'GB110-887R-A1'

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the BOM, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the BOM was created, defaults to now()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the BOM, references users(id)

columns.name: pcode_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the product code associated with the BOM, references pcodes(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the BOM. Must be unique

columns.name: sku
columns.description: The SKU generated based on the name of the BOM

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the BOM

tasks.description: Table containing task information. the base unit of work is a task - a task corresponds to a manual item of work who's outputs must be verified and recorded before the workflow can continue. Some common tasks include: (1) Choosing a log file path in P4 (2) Selecting a diag file in SharePoint (3) Signing off and leaving an optional comment on the log files from a previous task (4) Choosing a Nautilus branch and version, etc. Because all our tasks collect some form of input from the user, we can imagine them as HTML forms. react-jsonschema-form is a library for generating a React form for any data, simply given a JSON schema. This makes it a perfect choice for representing our library of tasks in a serializable and standardized way in the database. The @rjsf packages also give us the ability to define custom widgets for handling the presentation and interaction of individual form controls. This means that we could build a custom 'SharePoint File Selector' widget that actually browses SharePoint as opposed to using a plain text field. This makes our input collection less susceptible to typos or errors.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the task

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the task was created, defaults to now()

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the task

columns.name: category
columns.description: The category of the task

columns.name: json_schema
columns.description: The JSON schema defining the task structure

columns.name: ui_schema
columns.description: The UI schema defining the task's user interface

columns.name: supports_automation
columns.description: Indicates if the task supports automation. Defaults to false

columns.name: supports_collaboration
columns.description: Indicates if the task supports collaboration. Defaults to false

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the department, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the department was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the department, references users(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the department. Must be unique

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the department

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the stage, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the stage was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the stage, references users(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the stage. Must be unique

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the stage

workflows.description: Table containing workflow information. A workflow is modeled as a directed acyclic graph composed of tasks, arranged in a 2D matrix where the columns represent discrete stages in the request lifecycle, and rows represent the responsible departments. One workflow can be reused for a particular product or set of products. Workflows cannot be modified to add/remove tasks or change task dependencies if they have already been used in at least one request -- in order to do so, the user must clone the existing workflow and modify this one instead. This will allow us to maintain historical request traceability.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the workflow, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the workflow was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the workflow, references users(id)

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the workflow

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description of the workflow

workflow_departments.description: Table linking a workflow to a particular department. A workflow instance is composed of one or more multiple departments which are arranged in a particular order (by their rank) according to the workflow designer. As a result, this means that there is a many-to-many relationship between workflows and departments.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the workflow-department link, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the workflow-department link was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the workflow-department link, references users(id)

columns.name: workflow_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow, references workflows(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with department_id

columns.name: department_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the department, references departments(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with workflow_id

columns.name: rank
columns.description: The rank (i.e. index/position) of the department within the workflow

workflow_stages.description: Table linking a workflow to a particular stage. A workflow instance is composed of one or more multiple stages which are arranged in a particular order (by their rank) according to the workflow designer. As a result, this means that there is a many-to-many relationship between workflows and stages.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the workflow-stage link, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the workflow-stage link was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the workflow-stage link, references users(id)

columns.name: workflow_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow, references workflows(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with stage_id

columns.name: stage_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the stage, references stages(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with workflow_id

columns.name: rank
columns.description: The rank (i.e. index, position) of the stage within the workflow

workflow_tasks.description: A workflow task represents an instance of a task within a workflow and in particular, tells us which cell (i.e. row/department + column/stage) in the workflow matrix the task belongs. The workflow is divided into rows and columns, where the rows represent the workflow's relevant departments and the columns represent workflow's discrete stages

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the workflow-task link, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the workflow-task link was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the workflow-task link, references users(id)

columns.name: task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the task, references tasks(id)

columns.name: workflow_department_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow department, references workflow_departments(id). On delete cascade

columns.name: workflow_stage_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow stage, references workflow_stages(id). On delete cascade

workflow_task_edges.description: Workflow tasks can be connected together in any valid directed acyclic graph. This means that we need a separate table to track the relationships between them as a node (i.e. task) within the graph can have multiple parents and/or multiple children. Note, it is important to ensure that no cycles are introduced.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the workflow task edge, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the workflow task edge was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the workflow task edge, references users(id)

columns.name: parent_workflow_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the parent workflow task, references workflow_tasks(id). On delete cascade

columns.name: child_workflow_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the child workflow task, references workflow_tasks(id). On delete cascade

.filter_rule_groups.description: This table helps us group filter rules that should be handled identically. Ideally, we should use the wildcard matching capabilities of a single filter rule to capture as many products as possible. But there are cases where you need to select only a certain number of SKUs or certain number of pcodes within a particular product

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the filter rule group, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the filter rule group was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the filter rule group, references users(id)

columns.name: workflow_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow associated with the filter rule group, references workflows(id). When a user wants to delete a filter rule group, we will set workflow_id to null which will effectively tells the system to start ignoring this rule

filter_rules.description: When a user creates a new request, we need to know which workflow and task configuration (i.e. default PIC, estimated time cost, and weight) to use. This information should be established when a new product is registered in the system. However, it would be quite painful if this process had to be repeated for every single product variant that we support, which could be in the thousands due to the number of SKUs within a particular pcode. Additionally, for some business units, all of the products should have the same workflow and workflow task data. With filter rules, we can define a simple rule that says 'all products with bu = GPU should be handled identically.' We can also make exceptions for certain rules by defining one that is more specific. For instance, 'all products with bu = GPU AND product = Quadro should be handled differently'

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the filter rule, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the filter rule was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the filter rule, references users(id)

columns.name: filter_rule_group_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the filter rule group, references filter_rule_groups(id)

columns.name: bu_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the business unit, references bus(id). Part of a composite unique constraint

columns.name: product_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the product, references products(id). Part of a composite unique constraint

columns.name: pcode_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the product code, references pcodes(id). Part of a composite unique constraint

columns.name: sku
columns.description: The stock keeping unit (SKU). Part of a composite unique constraint

columns.name: diag_type_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic type, references diag_types(id). Part of a composite unique constraint

columns.name: diag_name_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic name, references diag_names(id). Part of a composite unique constraint

filter_rule_group_tasks.description: A filter rule group task adds an additional layer of detail on top of the workflow_task (which itself is another layer on top of task). In particular, these values include the default PIC (person-in-charge), weight, and time cost estimate. For instance, two different filter rule groups could have the exact same workflow DAG, but they might choose to have different default PICs or may choose to measure the weight of the same tasks in a different way. It is possible that a user can change which workflow the a rule group is configured to use, but this will not result in deleting it's filter rule group tasks. Rather, the user will be required to create new tasks and the existing tasks will remain in place to ensure that any requests that previously used this rule group can reconstruct their state

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the filter rule group task, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the filter rule group task was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the filter rule group task, references users(id)

columns.name: filter_rule_group_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the filter rule group, references filter_rule_groups(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with workflow_task_id

columns.name: workflow_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the workflow task, references workflow_tasks(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with filter_rule_group_id. On delete cascade

columns.name: pic_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the person in charge (PIC), references users(id). If left null, then the default PIC is dynamically assigned to the request creator at request time

columns.name: weight
columns.description: The weight (difficulty level) assigned to the filter rule group task

columns.name: time_cost_estimate
columns.description: The estimated time cost (in hours) for the filter rule group task. This value is currently constrained between 1 and 168 = (7 * 24)

collaborators.description: Table containing information about collaborators on filter rule group tasks. Task rows where supports_collaboration = TRUE allow additional performers besides the filter_rule_group_task.pic_id

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the collaborator, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the collaborator was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the collaborator entry, references users(id)

columns.name: filter_rule_group_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the filter rule group task, references filter_rule_group_tasks(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with user_id

columns.name: user_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who is a collaborator, references users(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with filter_rule_group_task_id

observers.description: Table containing information about observers for filter rule groups. Observers receive email + Slack notifications for all activity pertaining to a request that used that particular filter rule group as its template

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the observer, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the observer was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the observer entry, references users(id)

columns.name: filter_rule_group_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the filter rule group, references filter_rule_groups(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with user_id and group_id

columns.name: user_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who is an observer, references users(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with filter_rule_group_id and group_id. Additionally, there is a check constraint to ensure either user_id or group_id is not null, but not both

columns.name: group_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the group. This value is not a reference to any entity in our database, but rather an external ID for the group in Helios. Part of a composite unique constraint with filter_rule_group_id and user_id. Additionally, there is a check constraint to ensure either user_id or group_id is not null, but not both

requests.description: Table containing request information. Requests are the primary entity in the system. They are also referred to as DORs which stands for Diag-on-Request

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request, generated by default as identity starting with 50000

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request, references users(id)

columns.name: diag_type_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic type, references diag_types(id)

columns.name: diag_name_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic name, references diag_names(id)

columns.name: category_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the category, references categories(id)

columns.name: trainstop_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the train stop, references trainstops(id)

columns.name: quality_level_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the quality level, references quality_levels(id)

columns.name: process_ids
columns.description: An array of unique identifiers for the processes associated with the request

columns.name: description_text
columns.description: The text description of the request

columns.name: description_storage_key
columns.description: The blob storage key for the description of the request

columns.name: due_date
columns.description: The due date for the request

columns.name: status
columns.description: The status of the request

columns.name: revision
columns.description: The revision number of the request, defaults to 0. This value is incremented whenever a request's state goes from completed -> in_progress

columns.name: completed_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request was completed. This value is set to NOW() once the final request task corresponding to the request enters a terminal state

request_tasks.description: Table containing tasks associated with requests. When a request is created, we create a new row in this table for every filter rule group task belonging to the filter rule group template used for the request. The filter rule group task tells us the task weight, estimated time cost, and PIC. Of these values, only the PIC is changeable once the request is in flight

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request task, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request task was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request task, references users(id)

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with filter_rule_group_task_id

columns.name: filter_rule_group_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the filter rule group task, references filter_rule_group_tasks(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id

columns.name: pic_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the person in charge (PIC), references users(id). This is the current PIC of the request task. The default comes from the corresponding value of pic_id for the associated filter_rule_group_task_id.

columns.name: status
columns.description: The status of the request task. A completed task has been successfully submitted. A dismissed task has been skipped. Both of these are terminal. A failed status indicates that the task automation job did not succeed. The in_progress status means the task is in complete and is currently blocking the progress of other dependent tasks. The pending status is used for tasks that are unable to begin due to the fact that they are dependent (due to workflow_task_edges) on other tasks that have not arrived at a terminal state

columns.name: smartfarm_job_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the related Smartfarm job. This value is set if the task is automated

request_task_collaborators.description: Table containing collaborators for request tasks. This is the request-level analog of the request_task.pic_id -> filter_rule_group_task.pic_id. Once a request has been created, the collaborators of any task the support collaboration can be changed without impacting the base template defined in collaborators

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request task collaborator, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request task collaborator was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request task collaborator entry, references users(id)

columns.name: request_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task, references request_tasks(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with user_id

columns.name: user_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who is a collaborator, references users(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_task_id

request_task_events.description: Table containing events associated with request tasks. Not all task event types are related to a status change in the request task itself

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request task event, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request task event was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request task event, references users(id)

columns.name: request_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task, references request_tasks(id)

columns.name: triggered_by_event_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the event that triggered this request task event, references request_task_events(id). For example, submitting or dismissing a task can cause a dependent task to 'start' as its dependencies are now complete

columns.name: event_type
columns.description: The type of the event. 'abort', 'cancel', 'dispatch', 'fail', and 'retry' are all associated with automated request tasks. All request tasks get a 'create' event when the request is created. Tasks that have no dependencies also get a 'start' event at the same time. A task can be dismissed which may trigger dependent tasks to start. The inverse of 'dismiss' is 'restore' at which point any terminal that are dependent on the 'restore' target are 'reset' to a pending state. The 'escalate' event is used to nudge the performer via notification and additionally includes their manager. The 'urge' event is identical to 'escalate' but without the manager. The 'submit' and 'rollback' events are identical to the 'dismiss' and 'restore' events in their behavior, but 'submit' has real event data whereas 'dismiss' is a trivial skip. Finally, 'save' is the similar as 'submit' in that data is collected but it is used to retain state in between sessions for complex request tasks

columns.name: event_data
columns.description: Additional data associated with the event. Only 'reassign', 'save', and 'submit' events produce event_data. The value behind the 'formData' key is always compliant with the json_schema definition in the request_tasks underlying task definition. 'submit' events will also include a 'smartfarmJobId' key that is non-null when the submission comes from the automation bot as opposed to a human

columns.name: comment
columns.description: Any comments associated with the event

request_events.description: Table containing events associated with requests. At the moment, these events are only generated when the request status changes. An in_progress request can be abandoned part-way through. An in_progress request can also become completed when the final request task enters a terminal state. Similarly, a request can go from completed to in_progress when a task is reopened

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request event, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request event was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request event, references users(id)

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id)

columns.name: triggered_by_task_event_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the task event that triggered this request event, references request_task_events(id). The complete and reopen events are always caused by a state change in a request_task, hence the desire to track which task caused the change

columns.name: event_type
columns.description: The type of the event

request_boms.description: Table linking requests to BOMs (Bill of Materials). A request can target 1+ BOMs and we use this table to determine the relationship between a request and inventory as request -> request_boms -> boms -> pcodes -> products -> bus

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request BOM, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request BOM was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request BOM, references users(id)

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with bom_id

columns.name: bom_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the BOM, references boms(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id

request_task_fields.description: Table containing flattened 'formData' from 'save' and 'submit' request_task_events. It is a denormalized Entity-Attribute-Value (EAV) table which exists in order to efficiently identify requests by their task data. Because not all requests have the same workflow, they may collect different types of data.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request task field, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request task field was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with field_name

columns.name: request_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task, references request_tasks(id)

columns.name: request_task_event_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task event, references request_task_events(id). This request_task_event.event_type must be 'save' or 'submit'

columns.name: field_name
columns.description: The name of the field. Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id

columns.name: text_value
columns.description: The text value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, boolean_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: numeric_value
columns.description: The numeric value of the field. If the value is non-null, then text_value, boolean_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: boolean_value
columns.description: The boolean value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, text_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: array_value
columns.description: The array value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, boolean_value, and text_value must all be null

request_bom_task_fields.description: Table containing flattened 'formData' from 'save' and 'submit' request_task_events. It is a denormalized Entity-Attribute-Value (EAV) table which exists in order to efficiently identify requests by their BOM-related task data. Because not all requests have the same workflow, they may collect different types of data.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier (primary key) for the request BOM task field, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request BOM task field was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with bom_id, process_id, and field_name

columns.name: request_task_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task, references request_tasks(id)

columns.name: request_task_event_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request task event, references request_task_events(id)

columns.name: bom_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the BOM, references boms(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id, process_id, and field_name. Each row corresponds to a datum belonging to one BOM in the request

columns.name: process_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the process, references processes(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id, bom_id, and field_name. Each row may optionally include a process_id from the requests.process_ids column. When this occurs, then the same field_name + bom_id may occur multiple times but with a unique process_id for each row

columns.name: field_name
columns.description: The name of the custom field. Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id, bom_id, and process_id

columns.name: text_value
columns.description: The text value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, boolean_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: numeric_value
columns.description: The numeric value of the field. If the value is non-null, then text_value, boolean_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: boolean_value
columns.description: The boolean value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, text_value, and array_value must all be null

columns.name: array_value
columns.description: The array value of the field. If the value is non-null, then numeric_value, boolean_value, and text_value must all be null

request_observers.description: Table containing observers for requests. Observers receive email + Slack notifications for all activity pertaining to a request. The rows of this table are copied from the observers table when a request is created based on the filter rule group template. Once a request is in flight, observers can be added + removed without impacting the underlying template observers table

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier for the request observer, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request observer was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request observer entry, references users(id)

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with user_id and group_id

columns.name: user_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who is an observer, references users(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id and group_id

columns.name: group_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the group. Part of a composite unique constraint with request_id and user_id

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier for the request comment, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the request comment was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the request comment, references users(id)

columns.name: request_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the request, references requests(id)

columns.name: content_text
columns.description: The text content of the comment

columns.name: content_storage_key
columns.description: The blob storage key for the content of the comment

test_plans.description: Table containing test plan information. Test plans are used to define default process sequence options for a request creator.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier for the test plan, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the test plan was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the test plan, references users(id)

columns.name: pcode_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the product code, references pcodes(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with diag_type_id and diag_name_id

columns.name: diag_type_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic type, references diag_types(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with pcode_id and diag_name_id

columns.name: diag_name_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the diagnostic name, references diag_names(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with pcode_id and diag_type_id

test_plan_processes.description: Table linking test plans to processes with a specific rank (i.e. index/position)

columns.name: id
columns.description: The unique identifier for the test plan process, generated using gen_random_uuid()

columns.name: created_at
columns.description: The timestamp of when the test plan process was created, defaults to NOW()

columns.name: creator_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the user who created the test plan process entry, references users(id)

columns.name: test_plan_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the test plan, references test_plans(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with process_id. On delete cascade

columns.name: process_id
columns.description: The unique identifier of the process, references processes(id). Part of a composite unique constraint with test_plan_id

columns.name: rank
columns.description: The rank of the process within the test plan
